Details: Ultraviolet (UV) irradiation and ozone was reported to remove strychnine in water by 98 percent in one study [1489].  The UV and ozone unit was a component of a point-of-entry drinking water system. The system consisted of a reverse osmosis (RO) module, the UV and ozone module (named as advanced simultaneous oxidation process, or ASOP), followed by a granular activiated carbon (GAC) filter. The UV light output was 30,000 microwatt-seconds/cm2 with a contact time of 33 seconds. The ozone dose was not reported. The influent to the UV+ozone module contained 910 μg/L strychnine with the pH level between 7.7 to 7.8. The effluent strychnine concentration from the UV+ozone unit was 20 μg/L.
